title: Who drove the war sequence
summary: Trump suggested he may have forced Israel’s hand, while Rubio framed the move as the U.S. reacting to an expected Israeli action.
tension: If the trigger story changes, the implied legal and strategic rationale changes with it.

title: The obliterated-program problem
summary: Jeffries repeatedly argues that if Trump already said the nuclear program was obliterated, the same program cannot cleanly serve as the urgent basis for a new strike cycle.
tension: Either the earlier destruction claim was overstated, or the new emergency case is overstated.

title: Threat recognition versus war rejection
summary: Jeffries calls Iran a sworn enemy and bad actor while also arguing the administration showed zero evidence for imminent threat and lawful escalation.
tension: The profile is not pacifist; it is a split between accepting the danger and rejecting the legal-evidentiary pathway used to confront it.

title: Anti-war front versus hawkish Democratic minority
summary: A visible anti-war bloc pushed War Powers restrictions while a smaller Democratic hawkish lane rejected or diluted them.
tension: The archive cannot be scaled honestly unless party messaging is modeled as internally fragmented rather than monolithic.

title: Domestic-cost rhetoric versus spending record
summary: Fiscal irony files argue that leaders condemning war costs as anti-worker previously backed massive spending programs that critics say worsened affordability.
tension: This does not prove the anti-war case is false, but it creates a reusable consistency test for future issue tracking.

title: Leadership condemnation versus left dissatisfaction
summary: Left critics argue leadership rhetoric remained too delayed and procedural even after leadership publicly opposed the war path.
tension: A future observatory needs to track not just party-vs-party conflict, but elite-vs-movement divergence within the same coalition.
